items.q: What is the difference between CPI and RPI as inflation measures?
items.correct: Both measure price changes via a basket of goods, but RPI includes housing costs (mortgage interest) and uses an arithmetic mean; CPI excludes housing costs and uses a geometric mean — CPI is the official UK target measure.
items.misconception: CPI and RPI always give the same inflation figure.
items.random_wrong: CPI measures consumer prices and RPI measures retail prices — that's the only difference.
items.why: RPI usually runs higher than CPI due to formula and coverage differences — this matters hugely for pensions, rail fares and benefits. Students treat the two as identical and miss the policy implications of which is used.

items.q: What defines a pure public good?
items.correct: Non-rivalry (one person's use doesn't reduce others') and non-excludability (you can't stop people benefiting) — e.g. national defence, street lighting.
items.misconception: A public good is anything provided by the government.
items.random_wrong: A public good is anything that's free.
items.why: Government provides many private goods (e.g. NHS treatment is rival). The economic definition is about the GOOD's properties, not who provides it. Students confuse provider with technical category.

items.q: Why do economists generally argue against protectionism?
items.correct: Tariffs and quotas raise prices for consumers, protect inefficient industries, invite retaliation, and reduce overall welfare — even when they save specific jobs, the cost per job saved typically exceeds the wage.
items.misconception: Protectionism always makes a country richer by keeping money at home.
items.random_wrong: Protectionism means protecting national parks from development.
items.why: Trade is positive-sum in standard theory — countries gain from specialisation. Students assume tariffs trap wealth domestically, missing that consumers pay higher prices and exporters lose from retaliation.

items.q: A non-rival, non-excludable good is a:
items.why: It's Public good. “Private good” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Adverse selection. “Moral hazard” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year of Hardin's 'Tragedy of the Commons':
items.why: The real figure is 1968. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
